name: विश्वरूपदर्शनयोग
meaning: The Vision of the Cosmic Form
teaching: Arjuna asks to see — and is given eyes no mortal has. The universal form is not a comfort: it is all faces, all fires, all futures, armies streaming into blazing mouths, time itself declaring 'I am become Death, destroyer of worlds.' Arjuna, terrified, begs for the familiar face back. Some truth can be witnessed only briefly; love restores the human scale in which we can live. Theology becomes sight: the universal form blazes, devours, and overwhelms — and then mercy restores the gentle face. The vision's price is undivided love; its final lesson, friendship to every being.
questions: Arjuna learns the warriors are 'already slain' — he is only the instrument. Where might you be an instrument of something already in motion?
What truth have you glimpsed that was too large to hold — and what did you do with it?
Why do you think the vision ends with a request for the gentle, familiar form?

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
मदनुग्रहाय परमं गुह्यमध्यात्मसंज्ञितम् ।
यत्त्वयोक्तं वचस्तेन मोहोऽयं विगतो मम॥
verses.transliteration:
madanugrahāya paramaṃ guhyamadhyātmasaṃjñitam
yattvayoktaṃ vacastena moho'yaṃ vigato mama
verses.essence: Arjuna says: by your grace toward me, the supreme secret of the Self has been spoken — and my delusion is gone.
verses.translation: By this word (explanation) of the highest secret concerning the Self which Thou hast spoken, for the sake of blessing me, my delusion is gone.

anvaya.meaning: as a favor to me, the supreme
secret known as adhyatma
the word spoken by you — by that
this delusion of mine has departed

verses.explanation: Arjuna opens with gratitude and a claim he believes: moho 'yaṁ vigataḥ — the confusion is gone. He has heard ten chapters of doctrine and understood them. What this chapter will teach him is the distance between understanding a thing and seeing it.
verses.connection: Receipt for chapters 7–10 — the prelude to asking for more than words.
verses.reflection: Notice the gap in yourself between understanding a truth and having seen it — honor both stages.
verses.background: Arjuna, his delusion dissolved by the words just heard, makes the boldest request in scripture: You have spoken the supreme mystery and I have heard Your glories — now, if You think me able to bear it, O Lord of yoga, show me Your imperishable cosmic Self with my own eyes.

verses.devanagari:
भवाप्ययौ हि भूतानां श्रुतौ विस्तरशो मया ।
त्वत्तः कमलपत्राक्ष माहात्म्यमपि चाव्ययम्॥
verses.transliteration:
bhavāpyayau hi bhūtānāṃ śrutau vistaraśo mayā
tvattaḥ kamalapatrākṣa māhātmyamapi cāvyayam
verses.essence: The origin and dissolution of beings I have heard from you in detail — and also your imperishable greatness.
verses.translation: The origin and the destruction of beings verily have been heard by me in detail from Thee, O lotus-eyed Lord, and also Thy inexhaustible greatness.

anvaya.meaning: the origin and dissolution of beings
have been heard by me in detail
from you, O lotus-petal-eyed
and also (your) imperishable greatness

verses.explanation: The inventory of what he holds: cosmology, the births and ends of all beings, the catalogue of glories — all śrutau, *heard*. Note the address, kamala-patrākṣa, lotus-eyed: even while preparing to ask for the cosmic, he is looking at the gentle human face he knows.
verses.connection: Summarizes the vibhuti teaching of ch10 — heard, detailed, and not yet enough.
verses.reflection: Inventory what you have heard but not yet beheld — let the list become a prayer, not a complaint.
verses.background: Arjuna, his delusion dissolved by the words just heard, makes the boldest request in scripture: You have spoken the supreme mystery and I have heard Your glories — now, if You think me able to bear it, O Lord of yoga, show me Your imperishable cosmic Self with my own eyes.

verses.devanagari:
एवमेतद्यथात्थ त्वमात्मानं परमेश्वर ।
द्रष्टुमिच्छामि ते रूपमैश्वरं पुरुषोत्तम॥
verses.transliteration:
evametadyathāttha tvamātmānaṃ parameśvara
draṣṭumicchāmi te rūpamaiśvaraṃ puruṣottama
verses.essence: As you declare yourself, O supreme Lord, so it is. Yet I desire to see your lordly form, Purushottama.
verses.translation: (Now) O Supreme Lord, as Thou hast thus described Thyself, O Supreme Person, I wish to see Thy divine form.

anvaya.meaning: so is this, as you declare
yourself, O supreme Lord
I desire to see your form
the lordly one, O Purushottama

verses.explanation: The pivot of the whole Gita's middle: evam etat — I believe every word — *and* draṣṭum icchāmi, I want to see. Not doubt asking for proof, but love asking for presence; faith that has matured to the point of wanting its object unveiled. The boldest request a creature has made.
verses.connection: Converts ch10's heard glories into the demand for direct vision.
verses.reflection: Let your faith mature into Arjuna's boldness once: I believe You — and I long to see.
verses.background: Arjuna, his delusion dissolved by the words just heard, makes the boldest request in scripture: You have spoken the supreme mystery and I have heard Your glories — now, if You think me able to bear it, O Lord of yoga, show me Your imperishable cosmic Self with my own eyes.

verses.devanagari:
मन्यसे यदि तच्छक्यं मया द्रष्टुमिति प्रभो ।
योगेश्वर ततो मे त्वं दर्शयात्मानमव्ययम्॥
verses.transliteration:
manyase yadi tacchakyaṃ mayā draṣṭumiti prabho
yogeśvara tato me tvaṃ darśayātmānamavyayam
verses.essence: If you think it can be seen by me, O Lord — then, master of yoga, show me your imperishable Self.
verses.translation: If Thou, O Lord, thinkest it possible for me to see it, do Thou, then, O Lord of the Yogins, show me Thy imperishable Self.

anvaya.meaning: if you think that possible
to be seen by me, O Lord
then, O Lord of yoga, you to me
show the imperishable Self

verses.explanation: The request, perfectly mannered: yadi manyase — if *you* judge me able. Arjuna does not presume capacity; he submits his eyes for assessment. And he names the right office: yogeśvara, Lord of yoga — for what he asks requires not just willingness to show but power to make showable.
verses.connection: Completes 11.3's plea with the humility that makes granting it possible.
verses.reflection: Ask for the great things conditionally, as he did: if You judge me able — then show me.
verses.background: Arjuna, his delusion dissolved by the words just heard, makes the boldest request in scripture: You have spoken the supreme mystery and I have heard Your glories — now, if You think me able to bear it, O Lord of yoga, show me Your imperishable cosmic Self with my own eyes.

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
पश्य मे पार्थ रूपाणि शतशोऽथ सहस्रशः ।
नानाविधानि दिव्यानि नानावर्णाकृतीनि च॥
verses.transliteration:
paśya me pārtha rūpāṇi śataśo'tha sahasraśaḥ
nānāvidhāni divyāni nānāvarṇākṛtīni ca
verses.essence: The Blessed Lord says: behold my forms, Partha — by hundreds and by thousands, divine, of many kinds, many colors and shapes.
verses.translation: Behold, O Arjuna, forms of Mine, by the hundreds and thousands, of different sorts, divine, and of various colours and shapes.

anvaya.meaning: behold my forms, O Partha
by hundreds and by thousands
of many kinds, divine
and of many colors and shapes

verses.explanation: Consent, and immediately the grammar strains: paśya, see — but rūpāṇi, *forms*, plural by hundreds and thousands. Arjuna asked to see one supreme form; he is told the supreme form is a multitude. The first lesson of the vision arrives before the vision: the One does not come in ones.
verses.connection: The granting of 11.4 — with a plural that already exceeds the request.
verses.reflection: Prepare to be exceeded: ask the One for one thing and expect hundreds in answer.
verses.background: Krishna consents and exceeds the asking: behold My forms by the hundreds and thousands, all the gods and wonders never seen before, the whole universe standing as one in My body — but you cannot see Me with these eyes of yours; I give you the divine eye. Behold My sovereign yoga.

verses.devanagari:
पश्यादित्यान्वसून्रुद्रानश्विनौ मरुतस्तथा ।
बहून्यदृष्टपूर्वाणि पश्याश्चर्याणि भारत॥
verses.transliteration:
paśyādityānvasūnrudrānaśvinau marutastathā
bahūnyadṛṣṭapūrvāṇi paśyāścaryāṇi bhārata
verses.essence: Behold the Adityas, the Vasus, the Rudras, the two Ashvins, the Maruts. Behold many wonders never seen before, Bharata.
verses.translation: Behold the Adityas, the Vasus, the Rudras, the two Asvins and also the Maruts; behold many wonders never seen before, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: behold the Adityas, Vasus, Rudras
the two Ashvins, and the Maruts
many things never seen before
behold the wonders, O Bharata

verses.explanation: The guest list: every order of divinity Vedic ears had only chanted about — sun-gods, storm-hosts, the healers of heaven — now gathered into one viewing. Adṛṣṭa-pūrvāṇi, never before seen: the phrase will toll through this chapter; Arjuna is being shown what no eye has held.
verses.connection: Itemizes 11.5's thousands: the whole pantheon, in one body.
verses.reflection: Expect wonders never seen before in the most familiar quarter of your life today.
verses.background: Krishna consents and exceeds the asking: behold My forms by the hundreds and thousands, all the gods and wonders never seen before, the whole universe standing as one in My body — but you cannot see Me with these eyes of yours; I give you the divine eye. Behold My sovereign yoga.

verses.devanagari:
इहैकस्थं जगत्कृत्स्नं पश्याद्य सचराचरम् ।
मम देहे गुडाकेश यच्चान्यद् द्रष्टुमिच्छसि॥
verses.transliteration:
ihaikasthaṃ jagatkṛtsnaṃ paśyādya sacarācaram
mama dehe guḍākeśa yaccānyad draṣṭumicchasi
verses.essence: Behold today the entire universe, moving and unmoving, standing as one here in my body — and whatever else you wish to see.
verses.translation: Now behold, O Arjuna, in this, My body, the whole universe centred in one including the moving and the unmoving and whatever else thou desirest to see.

anvaya.meaning: here, standing in one place, the whole universe
behold today, with its moving and unmoving
in my body, O Gudakesha
and whatever else you wish to see

verses.explanation: The impossible offer: eka-stham, the whole of everything in one place — all space without losing its vastness, gathered into a single standing. And then the blank check: whatever else you wish to see. The vision will include even what Arjuna has not thought to ask about — including, terribly, the war's outcome.
verses.connection: Raises 11.6's pantheon to totality: not gods in a body, but the universe in one.
verses.reflection: Hold the thought a moment: the whole universe, standing in one body — and that body near you.
verses.background: Krishna consents and exceeds the asking: behold My forms by the hundreds and thousands, all the gods and wonders never seen before, the whole universe standing as one in My body — but you cannot see Me with these eyes of yours; I give you the divine eye. Behold My sovereign yoga.

verses.devanagari:
न तु मां शक्यसे द्रष्टुमनेनैव स्वचक्षुषा ।
दिव्यं ददामि ते चक्षुः पश्य मे योगमैश्वरम्॥
verses.transliteration:
na tu māṃ śakyase draṣṭumanenaiva svacakṣuṣā
divyaṃ dadāmi te cakṣuḥ paśya me yogamaiśvaram
verses.essence: But you cannot see me with this eye of your own. I give you a divine eye: behold my sovereign yoga.
verses.translation: But thou art not able to behold Me with these thine own eyes; I give thee the divine eye; behold My lordly Yoga.

anvaya.meaning: but you are not able to see me
with this very eye of your own
a divine eye I give to you
behold my sovereign yoga

verses.explanation: The technical clause of grace: the instrument must be replaced before the object can appear. Divyaṁ dadāmi te cakṣuḥ — the vision is not earned, climbed to, or unlocked; it is *given*, eye and all. Every genuine revelation has this structure: the seeing itself is part of the gift.
verses.connection: Answers 11.4's 'if I am able': you are not — so I will make you able.
verses.reflection: Admit which seeings are beyond your current eyes — and ask for the eye, not just the view.
verses.background: Krishna consents and exceeds the asking: behold My forms by the hundreds and thousands, all the gods and wonders never seen before, the whole universe standing as one in My body — but you cannot see Me with these eyes of yours; I give you the divine eye. Behold My sovereign yoga.

verses.speaker: सञ्जय उवाच
verses.devanagari:
एवमुक्त्वा ततो राजन्महायोगेश्वरो हरिः ।
दर्शयामास पार्थाय परमं रूपमैश्वरम्॥
verses.transliteration:
evamuktvā tato rājanmahāyogeśvaro hariḥ
darśayāmāsa pārthāya paramaṃ rūpamaiśvaram
verses.essence: Sanjaya says: having spoken thus, O King, the great Lord of yoga, Hari, showed to Partha the supreme sovereign form.
verses.translation: Having thus spoken, O king, the great Lord of Yoga, hari (Krishna), showed to Arjuna His supreme form as the Lord.

anvaya.meaning: having spoken thus, then, O King
the great Lord of yoga, Hari
showed to Partha
the supreme sovereign form

verses.explanation: The narrative frame returns — we are reminded that blind Dhritarashtra is hearing all this secondhand in a palace. Sanjaya, gifted his own divine sight by Vyasa, becomes our witness's witness: the most overwhelming vision in scripture reaches us through a chain of borrowed eyes.
verses.connection: Hands the telling to Sanjaya for the vision proper (11.9–14): what words can carry of it.
verses.reflection: Remember the chain of witnesses that carried this vision to you — and become a faithful link.
verses.background: Sanjaya takes over the telling, for Arjuna is past speech: the great Lord of yoga reveals the supreme form — countless mouths and eyes, divine ornaments and weapons, facing every direction, with the radiance of a thousand suns rising at once. Arjuna, hair standing on end, bows his head and joins his palms.

verses.devanagari:
अनेकवक्त्रनयनमनेकाद्भुतदर्शनम् ।
अनेकदिव्याभरणं दिव्यानेकोद्यतायुधम्॥
verses.transliteration:
anekavaktranayanamanekādbhutadarśanam
anekadivyābharaṇaṃ divyānekodyatāyudham
verses.essence: Of many mouths and eyes, of many wondrous sights, of many divine ornaments, with many divine weapons uplifted —
verses.translation: With numerous mouths and eyes, with numerous wonderful sights, with numerous divine ornaments, with numerous divine weapons uplifted (such a form He showed).

anvaya.meaning: having many mouths and eyes
many wondrous sights
many divine ornaments
many divine weapons uplifted

verses.explanation: Language begins to fail in the only honest way: by repetition. Aneka, aneka, aneka — many, many, many — the word strikes four times because counting has already broken down. Mouths and eyes everywhere: nothing in this form has a back, a blind side, an away.
verses.connection: The first stroke of Sanjaya's description: multiplicity past numbering.
verses.reflection: Let one glimpse of the world's manyness today overwhelm your counting — and stand in the surplus.
verses.background: Sanjaya takes over the telling, for Arjuna is past speech: the great Lord of yoga reveals the supreme form — countless mouths and eyes, divine ornaments and weapons, facing every direction, with the radiance of a thousand suns rising at once. Arjuna, hair standing on end, bows his head and joins his palms.

verses.devanagari:
दिव्यमाल्याम्बरधरं दिव्यगन्धानुलेपनम् ।
सर्वाश्चर्यमयं देवमनन्तं विश्वतोमुखम्॥
verses.transliteration:
divyamālyāmbaradharaṃ divyagandhānulepanam
sarvāścaryamayaṃ devamanantaṃ viśvatomukham
verses.essence: Wearing divine garlands and garments, anointed with divine perfumes — all-wonderful, resplendent, boundless, with faces on every side.
verses.translation: Wearing divine garlands (necklaces) and apparel, anointed with divine unguents, the all-wonderful, resplendent (Being) endless with faces on all sides.

anvaya.meaning: wearing divine garlands and garments
anointed with divine perfumes
the god made all of wonder
infinite, facing everywhere

verses.explanation: Amid the overwhelming, tenderness: garlands, silks, sandal-fragrance — the universal form arrives *adorned*, beauty insisted upon even at the scale of everything. Then the two words that define it: anantam, without end, and viśvato-mukham, facing all directions at once — no one stands behind this God.
verses.connection: Completes the form's first portrait: terrifying scale, deliberately beautiful.
verses.reflection: Notice beauty insisting on itself even at terrifying scale — garlands on the infinite.
verses.background: Sanjaya takes over the telling, for Arjuna is past speech: the great Lord of yoga reveals the supreme form — countless mouths and eyes, divine ornaments and weapons, facing every direction, with the radiance of a thousand suns rising at once. Arjuna, hair standing on end, bows his head and joins his palms.

verses.devanagari:
दिवि सूर्यसहस्रस्य भवेद्युगपदुत्थिता ।
यदि भाः सदृशी सा स्याद्भासस्तस्य महात्मनः॥
verses.transliteration:
divi sūryasahasrasya bhavedyugapadutthitā
yadi bhāḥ sadṛśī sā syādbhāsastasya mahātmanaḥ
verses.essence: If the radiance of a thousand suns were to burst forth at once in the sky — that might resemble the splendor of that great Self.
verses.translation: If the splendour of a thousand suns were to blaze out at once (simultaneously) in the sky, that would be the splendour of that mighty Being (great Soul).

anvaya.meaning: of a thousand suns, in the sky
were to rise simultaneously
if such a light arose, it might be like
the splendor of that great Self

verses.explanation: The most famous simile in the poem, and grammar keeps it humble: yadi... syāt — *if* a thousand suns rose at once, that *might* approach it. A comparison that confesses failure inside its own construction. Millennia later, watching the first atomic fire, Oppenheimer reached for this verse — the only image in human inventory sized for what he saw. Divi surya-sahasrasya — the thousand-suns verse, which crossed into world history when Oppenheimer reached for it at Trinity. The tradition reads the simile's own confession: yadi... syat, 'if such a light should rise, it might be like' — even hypothetical infinity only approximates. Sanjaya, granted divine sight, still files his report as a failed comparison; that failure is the description.
verses.connection: Sanjaya's reach for a measure — and the admission that no measure holds.
verses.reflection: Recall the thousand-suns line at your next sunrise — one sun, and the comparison already fails.
verses.background: Sanjaya takes over the telling, for Arjuna is past speech: the great Lord of yoga reveals the supreme form — countless mouths and eyes, divine ornaments and weapons, facing every direction, with the radiance of a thousand suns rising at once. Arjuna, hair standing on end, bows his head and joins his palms.

verses.devanagari:
तत्रैकस्थं जगत्कृत्स्नं प्रविभक्तमनेकधा ।
अपश्यद्देवदेवस्य शरीरे पाण्डवस्तदा॥
verses.transliteration:
tatraikasthaṃ jagatkṛtsnaṃ pravibhaktamanekadhā
apaśyaddevadevasya śarīre pāṇḍavastadā
verses.essence: There, in the body of the God of gods, the Pandava then saw the whole universe standing as one, divided into its many kinds.
verses.translation: There, in the body of the God of gods, Arjuna then saw the whole universe resting in one, with its many groups.

anvaya.meaning: there, standing as one, the whole universe
divided manifold
saw — of the God of gods
in the body, the Pandava, then

verses.explanation: The paradox the divine eye was issued for: eka-sthaṁ... pravibhaktam anekadhā — all things in one place, yet each thing distinct; unity that does not blur, multiplicity that does not scatter. Ordinary sight must choose between the forest and the trees. Arjuna, for this hour, does not.
verses.connection: Fulfills 11.7's promise exactly — and shows why 11.8's new eye was required.
verses.reflection: Practice seeing one-in-many today: the family in its members, the forest in its trees, undivided.
verses.background: Sanjaya takes over the telling, for Arjuna is past speech: the great Lord of yoga reveals the supreme form — countless mouths and eyes, divine ornaments and weapons, facing every direction, with the radiance of a thousand suns rising at once. Arjuna, hair standing on end, bows his head and joins his palms.

verses.devanagari:
ततः स विस्मयाविष्टो हृष्टरोमा धनञ्जयः ।
प्रणम्य शिरसा देवं कृताञ्जलिरभाषत॥
verses.transliteration:
tataḥ sa vismayāviṣṭo hṛṣṭaromā dhanañjayaḥ
praṇamya śirasā devaṃ kṛtāñjalirabhāṣata
verses.essence: Then Dhananjaya, struck with amazement, his hair standing on end, bowed his head to the God and spoke with palms joined.
verses.translation: Then, Arjuna, filled with wonder and with his hair standing on end, bowed down his head to the God and spoke with joined palms.

anvaya.meaning: then he, filled with amazement
his hair thrilling erect, Dhananjaya
bowing with his head to the God
with joined palms, spoke

verses.explanation: The body answers before the mind can: hṛṣṭa-romā, every hair erect — awe registered in the skin. And the warrior's hands, trained for the bow, find their oldest gesture instead: kṛtāñjali, the añjali of palms joined. What follows (11.15–31) is not analysis but hymn — the only speech possible at this altitude.
verses.connection: The hinge from Sanjaya's report to Arjuna's own voice — description giving way to prayer.
verses.reflection: Let awe register in the body once today — gooseflesh, stilled breath — and bow instead of analyzing.
verses.background: Sanjaya takes over the telling, for Arjuna is past speech: the great Lord of yoga reveals the supreme form — countless mouths and eyes, divine ornaments and weapons, facing every direction, with the radiance of a thousand suns rising at once. Arjuna, hair standing on end, bows his head and joins his palms.

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
पश्यामि देवांस्तव देव देहे
सर्वांस्तथा भूतविशेषसङ्घान् ।
ब्रह्माणमीशं कमलासनस्थ-
मृषींश्च सर्वानुरगांश्च दिव्यान्॥
verses.transliteration:
paśyāmi devāṃstava deva dehe sarvāṃstathā bhūtaviśeṣasaṅghān
brahmāṇamīśaṃ kamalāsanasthaṃ ṛṣīṃśca sarvānuragāṃśca divyān
verses.essence: Arjuna says: I see the gods in your body, O God, and all the hosts of beings — Brahma on his lotus throne, all the seers, and the shining serpents.
verses.translation: I see all the gods, O God, in Thy body, and (also) hosts of various classes of beings, Brahma, the Lord, seated on the lotus, all the sages and the celestial serpents.

anvaya.meaning: I see the gods in your body, O God
likewise all the classes of beings
Brahma the lord, seated on the lotus

anvaya.word: ऋषीन् च सर्वान् उरगान् च दिव्यान्
anvaya.meaning: and all the seers, and the divine serpents

verses.explanation: Arjuna's hymn begins, and the meter itself changes — the verses lengthen, as if the lines must stretch to hold what the eyes are receiving. First sight: the creator himself, Brahma, *inside* this body — the maker of worlds contained in what stands on Arjuna's chariot-field.
verses.connection: Opens the eyewitness hymn (11.15–31): the seen, in the seer's own words.
verses.reflection: When the vision comes, in any size, do what Arjuna does: start naming what you see, gratefully.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
अनेकबाहूदरवक्त्रनेत्रं
पश्यामि त्वां सर्वतोऽनन्तरूपम् ।
नान्तं न मध्यं न पुनस्तवादिं
पश्यामि विश्वेश्वर विश्वरूप॥
verses.transliteration:
anekabāhūdaravaktranetraṃ paśyāmi tvāṃ sarvato'nantarūpam
nāntaṃ na madhyaṃ na punastavādiṃ paśyāmi viśveśvara viśvarūpa
verses.essence: With many arms, bellies, mouths and eyes, I see your infinite form on every side — no end, no middle, no beginning of you do I see, Lord of all, form of all.
verses.translation: I see Thee of boundless form on every side with many arms, stomachs, mouths and eyes: neither the end nor the middle nor also the beginning do I see, O Lord of the universe, O Cosmic Form.

anvaya.meaning: with many arms, bellies, mouths, eyes
I see you, on every side, of infinite form

anvaya.word: न अन्तं न मध्यं न पुनः तव आदिं
anvaya.meaning: no end, no middle, nor again your beginning
do I see, O Lord of all, O all-formed

verses.explanation: Geometry surrenders: a form with no edge to start measuring from, no center to stand at, no boundary to finish at. Na antaṁ na madhyaṁ na ādim — the triple negation is exact: infinity cannot be surveyed, only confessed. The titles arrive fused: viśveśvara viśvarūpa — the Lord *of* all who is also the form *of* all.
verses.connection: The hymn's first vertigo: scale without coordinates.
verses.reflection: Stand before something with no findable edge today — sky, sea, love — and let geometry surrender.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
किरीटिनं गदिनं चक्रिणं च
तेजोराशिं सर्वतो दीप्तिमन्तम् ।
पश्यामि त्वां दुर्निरीक्ष्यं समन्ताद्
दीप्तानलार्कद्युतिमप्रमेयम्॥
verses.transliteration:
kirīṭinaṃ gadinaṃ cakriṇaṃ ca tejorāśiṃ sarvato dīptimantam
paśyāmi tvāṃ durnirīkṣyaṃ samantād dīptānalārkadyutimaprameyam
verses.essence: With crown, with mace, with discus — a mass of radiance blazing on every side I see you: hard to look upon, immeasurable, with the glare of blazing fire and sun.
verses.translation: I see Thee with the diadem, the club and the discus, a mass of radiance shining everywhere, very hard to look at, blazing all round like burning fire and the sun, and immeasurable.

anvaya.meaning: crowned, bearing mace and discus
a mass of splendor, blazing everywhere
I see you, hard to gaze upon, all around
with the light of flaming fire and sun, immeasurable

verses.explanation: For one moment the familiar iconography surfaces — crown, mace, discus, Vishnu's emblems — then drowns in tejo-rāśi, sheer mass of light. Durnirīkṣyam: hard to keep looking at. The divine eye granted in 11.8 lets Arjuna see; nothing was promised about the seeing being bearable.
verses.connection: The form's recognizable center glimpsed — and immediately swallowed by its own radiance.
verses.reflection: Respect the moments when looking is hard — some radiance is meant to overwhelm, not to be mastered.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
त्वमक्षरं परमं वेदितव्यं
त्वमस्य विश्वस्य परं निधानम् ।
त्वमव्ययः शाश्वतधर्मगोप्ता
सनातनस्त्वं पुरुषो मतो मे॥
verses.transliteration:
tvamakṣaraṃ paramaṃ veditavyaṃ tvamasya viśvasya paraṃ nidhānam
tvamavyayaḥ śāśvatadharmagoptā sanātanastvaṃ puruṣo mato me
verses.essence: You are the Imperishable, the supreme to be known; you are the final resting-place of all this; you are the undying guardian of the eternal dharma; you are, I hold, the primal Person.
verses.translation: Thou art the Imperishable, the Supreme Being, worthy to be known. Thou art the great treasure-house of this universe; Thou art the imperishable protector of the eternal Dhrama; Thou art the Primal Person, I deem.

anvaya.meaning: you are the Imperishable, the supreme to be known
you are the supreme repository of this universe
you are the undying guardian of eternal dharma
you are the eternal Person — so I hold

verses.explanation: Amid the blaze, theology: Arjuna recognizes in the vision everything the Upanishads taught him to seek — the Akshara, the supreme knowable, the world's final shelf. And one title with private weight: śāśvata-dharma-goptā, dharma's eternal guard. The man who feared this war would destroy dharma is looking at the one who keeps it.
verses.connection: The hymn's doctrinal core: the seen identified with everything scripture had named.
verses.reflection: Anchor in the middle of any storm today: You are the imperishable, the guardian of what endures.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
अनादिमध्यान्तमनन्तवीर्य-
मनन्तबाहुं शशिसूर्यनेत्रम् ।
पश्यामि त्वां दीप्तहुताशवक्त्रं
स्वतेजसा विश्वमिदं तपन्तम्॥
verses.transliteration:
anādimadhyāntamanantavīryam anantabāhuṃ śaśisūryanetram
paśyāmi tvāṃ dīptahutāśavaktraṃ svatejasā viśvamidaṃ tapantam
verses.essence: Without beginning, middle or end, of infinite power, of endless arms, with sun and moon for eyes — I see you, your mouth a blazing fire, scorching this universe with your radiance.
verses.translation: I see Thee without beginning, middle or end, infinite in power, of endless arms, the sun and the moon being Thy eyes, the burning fire Thy mouth, heating the whole universe with Thy radiance.

anvaya.meaning: without beginning, middle, end; of infinite power
of endless arms, with moon and sun for eyes
I see you, your mouth a blazing fire
heating this universe with your own splendor

verses.explanation: The cosmos rearranges into anatomy: sun and moon demoted to a pair of eyes, and the mouth — dīpta-hutāśa-vaktram, a blazing sacrificial fire. The image is precise and ominous: a hutāśa is the fire that *consumes offerings*. Arjuna does not yet know what offerings this mouth is about to receive.
verses.connection: Plants the fire-mouth image that 11.26–30 will turn from glory to horror.
verses.reflection: See sun and moon once today as eyes — and feel yourself watched by warmth.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
द्यावापृथिव्योरिदमन्तरं हि
व्याप्तं त्वयैकेन दिशश्च सर्वाः ।
दृष्ट्वाद्भुतं रूपमुग्रं तवेदं
लोकत्रयं प्रव्यथितं महात्मन्॥
verses.transliteration:
dyāvāpṛthivyoridamantaraṃ hi vyāptaṃ tvayaikena diśaśca sarvāḥ
dṛṣṭvādbhutaṃ rūpamugraṃ tavedaṃ lokatrayaṃ pravyathitaṃ mahātman
verses.essence: This space between heaven and earth is filled by you alone, and all the directions. Seeing this wondrous, terrible form of yours, the three worlds tremble, O great Self.
verses.translation: This space between the earth and the heaven and all the arters are filled by Thee alone; having seen this, Thy wonderful and teriible form, the three worlds are trembling with fear, O great-souled Being.

anvaya.meaning: for this interval between heaven and earth

anvaya.word: व्याप्तं त्वया एकेन दिशः च सर्वाः
anvaya.meaning: is pervaded by you alone, and all the directions

anvaya.word: दृष्ट्वा अद्भुतं रूपम् उग्रं तव इदं
anvaya.meaning: seeing this wondrous, fierce form of yours
the three worlds tremble, O great Self

verses.explanation: Two words now travel together that the hymn cannot separate: adbhutam ugram — wondrous *and* terrible. And the audience widens: loka-trayaṁ pravyathitam, the three worlds themselves are shaking. This is no private vision; whatever Arjuna is seeing, all of reality is feeling.
verses.connection: The hymn turns: wonder begins its slide toward dread.
verses.reflection: When dread and wonder arrive together today, refuse to choose — adbhutam ugram, both are true.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
अमी हि त्वां सुरसङ्घा विशन्ति
केचिद्भीताः प्राञ्जलयो गृणन्ति ।
स्वस्तीत्युक्त्वा महर्षिसिद्धसङ्घाः
स्तुवन्ति त्वां स्तुतिभिः पुष्कलाभिः॥
verses.transliteration:
amī hi tvāṃ surasaṅghā viśanti kecidbhītāḥ prāñjalayo gṛṇanti
svastītyuktvā maharṣisiddhasaṅghāḥ stuvanti tvāṃ stutibhiḥ puṣkalābhiḥ
verses.essence: These hosts of gods enter into you; some, terrified, praise you with joined palms. Crying 'Hail!', the throngs of great seers and siddhas praise you with abundant hymns.
verses.translation: Verily, into Thee enter these hosts of gods; some extol Thee in fear with joined palms; saying 'may it be well', bands of great sages and perfected ones praise Thee with hymns complete.

anvaya.meaning: these hosts of gods enter into you
some, afraid, with joined palms praise
crying 'svasti!', the throngs of maharshis and siddhas
praise you with abundant hymns

verses.explanation: The traffic of the vision is one-way: viśanti, the gods *enter* him — streams of divinity flowing back into their source. Even heaven's population divides by temperament: some flee into praise, the seers cry svasti — 'let there be well-being' — the word one says when well-being is suddenly in question.
verses.connection: The pantheon of 11.6 now seen in motion: not displayed in him but returning into him.
verses.reflection: Watch everything streaming back into its source — rivers, days, lives — and practice not clutching.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
रुद्रादित्या वसवो ये च साध्या
विश्वेऽश्विनौ मरुतश्चोष्मपाश्च ।
गन्धर्वयक्षासुरसिद्धसङ्घा
वीक्षन्ते त्वां विस्मिताश्चैव सर्वे॥
verses.transliteration:
rudrādityā vasavo ye ca sādhyā viśve'śvinau marutaścoṣmapāśca
gandharvayakṣāsurasiddhasaṅghā vīkṣante tvāṃ vismitāścaiva sarve
verses.essence: The Rudras, Adityas, Vasus and Sadhyas, the Vishvas, the Ashvins, Maruts and ancestors, the hosts of gandharvas, yakshas, asuras and siddhas — all gaze upon you, amazed.
verses.translation: The Rudras, Adityas, Vasus, Sadhyas, Visvedevas, the two Asvins, Maruts, the manes and the hosts of celestial singers, Yakshas, demons and the perfected ones, are all looking at Thee, in great amazement.

anvaya.meaning: the Rudras, Adityas, Vasus and Sadhyas

anvaya.word: विश्वे अश्विनौ मरुतः च ऊष्मपाः च
anvaya.meaning: the Vishvas, the two Ashvins, Maruts and ancestors
the hosts of gandharvas, yakshas, asuras, siddhas

anvaya.word: वीक्षन्ते त्वां विस्मिताः च एव सर्वे
anvaya.meaning: all gaze at you, amazed indeed

verses.explanation: The complete roll-call of the invisible world — gods, ancestors, celestial musicians, nature-spirits, even the asuras, heaven's opposition — and one verb for all of them: vīkṣante vismitāḥ, staring in astonishment. Whatever their rank or allegiance, before this form every being in the cosmos is reduced to the same expression.
verses.connection: Universalizes 11.21: nothing in existence is exempt from this audience.
verses.reflection: Remember that every order of being shares your astonishment — amazement is universal company.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
रूपं महत्ते बहुवक्त्रनेत्रं
महाबाहो बहुबाहूरुपादम् ।
बहूदरं बहुदंष्ट्राकरालं
दृष्ट्वा लोकाः प्रव्यथितास्तथाहम्॥
verses.transliteration:
rūpaṃ mahatte bahuvaktranetraṃ mahābāho bahubāhūrupādam
bahūdaraṃ bahudaṃṣṭrākarālaṃ dṛṣṭvā lokāḥ pravyathitāstathāham
verses.essence: Seeing your great form of many mouths and eyes, of many arms, thighs and feet, many bellies, terrible with many tusks — the worlds tremble, and so do I.
verses.translation: Having seen Thy immeasurable form with many mouths and eyes, O mighty-armed, with many arms, thighs and feet, with many stomachs and fearful with many teeth the worlds are terrified and so am I.

anvaya.meaning: your great form of many mouths and eyes
O mighty-armed, of many arms, thighs, feet
of many bellies, dreadful with many tusks
seeing it, the worlds tremble — and so do I

verses.explanation: A new detail enters the inventory and changes its tone: daṁṣṭrā, tusks — the form has fangs. And then the bravest small phrase in the hymn: tathā aham, *and so do I*. The greatest archer alive, who has never named his own fear, names it. The vision has found the bottom of his courage.
verses.connection: The dread becomes personal: from 'the worlds tremble' to 'I tremble.'
verses.reflection: When you finally tremble, say so plainly: and so do I. Honesty at the edge is also courage.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
नभःस्पृशं दीप्तमनेकवर्णं
व्यात्ताननं दीप्तविशालनेत्रम् ।
दृष्ट्वा हि त्वां प्रव्यथितान्तरात्मा
धृतिं न विन्दामि शमं च विष्णो॥
verses.transliteration:
nabhaḥspṛśaṃ dīptamanekavarṇaṃ vyāttānanaṃ dīptaviśālanetram
dṛṣṭvā hi tvāṃ pravyathitāntarātmā dhṛtiṃ na vindāmi śamaṃ ca viṣṇo
verses.essence: Touching the sky, blazing, many-colored, mouths agape, with vast flaming eyes — seeing you, my inmost self trembles, and I find neither steadiness nor calm, O Vishnu.
verses.translation: On seeing Thee (the Cosmic Form) touching the sky, shining in many colours, with mouths wide open, with large fiery eyes, I am terrified at heart and find neither courage nor peace, O Vishnu.

anvaya.meaning: touching the sky, blazing, many-colored
with mouths gaping open, with flaming vast eyes
seeing you, my inner self trembling

anvaya.word: धृतिं न विन्दामि शमं च विष्णो
anvaya.meaning: I find neither firmness nor peace, O Vishnu

verses.explanation: Vyāttānanam — mouths *opened wide*: the fire-mouths of 11.19 are no longer emblematic; they are gaping. Arjuna's report is clinical in its honesty: dhṛti and śama, the two pillars every previous chapter taught him — steadiness, calm — na vindāmi, I cannot find them. The curriculum has met something its exercises did not cover.
verses.connection: The vision overruns the very equanimity chapters 2–6 had built.
verses.reflection: When your trained steadiness fails before something vast, let it fail honestly — then pray.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
दंष्ट्राकरालानि च ते मुखानि
दृष्ट्वैव कालानलसन्निभानि ।
दिशो न जाने न लभे च शर्म
प्रसीद देवेश जगन्निवास॥
verses.transliteration:
daṃṣṭrākarālāni ca te mukhāni dṛṣṭvaiva kālānalasannibhāni
diśo na jāne na labhe ca śarma prasīda deveśa jagannivāsa
verses.essence: Seeing your mouths, terrible with tusks, like the fires of time's ending — I lose the directions and find no shelter. Be gracious, Lord of gods, dwelling of the universe!
verses.translation: Having seen Thy mouths fearful with teeth (blazing) like the fires of cosmic dissolution, I know not the four arters, nor do I find peace. Have mercy, O Lord of the gods, O abode of the universe.

anvaya.meaning: and your mouths, dreadful with tusks
seeing them, like the fires of cosmic dissolution

anvaya.word: दिशः न जाने न लभे च शर्म
anvaya.meaning: I know not the directions, I find no refuge
be gracious, Lord of gods, abode of the world

verses.explanation: The simile names what the mouths are: kālānala, the fire of Time at the world's end. Diśo na jāne — the warrior who navigates battlefields by the directions has lost them; there is no 'away' from a form that faces everywhere. And so the hymn breaks into its first cry: prasīda — mercy. Praise has run out; petition begins.
verses.connection: The turning word of the chapter: from describing the vision to begging relief from it.
verses.reflection: In your most lost hour, use Arjuna's words: I know not the directions — be gracious.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
अमी च त्वां धृतराष्ट्रस्य पुत्राः
सर्वे सहैवावनिपालसङ्घैः ।
भीष्मो द्रोणः सूतपुत्रस्तथासौ
सहास्मदीयैरपि योधमुख्यैः॥
verses.transliteration:
amī ca tvāṃ dhṛtarāṣṭrasya putrāḥ sarve sahaivāvanipālasaṅghaiḥ
bhīṣmo droṇaḥ sūtaputrastathāsau sahāsmadīyairapi yodhamukhyaiḥ
verses.essence: And all these sons of Dhritarashtra, together with the hosts of kings — Bhishma, Drona, and that son of a charioteer too, along with our own chief warriors —
verses.translation: All the sons of Dhritarashtra, with the hosts of kings of the earth, Bhishma, Drona and Karna, with the chief among our warriors.

anvaya.meaning: and these sons of Dhritarashtra — into you
all of them, together with the hosts of kings
Bhishma, Drona, and that charioteer's son (Karna)
along with our own chief warriors as well

verses.explanation: The vision pivots from cosmos to Kurukshetra: suddenly, inside the universal form, Arjuna recognizes *faces* — the very men arrayed outside it. Bhishma, Drona, Karna... and the sentence's most terrible word is its quietest: asmadīyaiḥ, *ours too*. Both armies. The vision is not taking sides.
verses.connection: The war enters the vision — chapter 1's named beloveds reappearing inside the fire.
verses.reflection: Look once today at both sides of your conflict inside one frame — the vision takes no sides.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
वक्त्राणि ते त्वरमाणा विशन्ति
दंष्ट्राकरालानि भयानकानि ।
केचिद्विलग्ना दशनान्तरेषु
सन्दृश्यन्ते चूर्णितैरुत्तमाङ्गैः॥
verses.transliteration:
vaktrāṇi te tvaramāṇā viśanti daṃṣṭrākarālāni bhayānakāni
kecidvilagnā daśanāntareṣu sandṛśyante cūrṇitairuttamāṅgaiḥ
verses.essence: — rush into your mouths, terrible with their tusks. Some are seen caught between your teeth, their heads crushed to powder.
verses.translation: Some hurriedly enter Thy mouths with their terrible teeth, fearful to behold. Some are found sticking in the gaps between the teeth with their heads crushed to powder.

anvaya.meaning: into your mouths they rush, hurrying
dreadful with tusks, terrifying
some, caught between the teeth
are seen, with their heads crushed

verses.explanation: Tvaramāṇāḥ — *hurrying*: the warriors are not dragged; they rush, as if eager, as if the rushing were their own idea. This is the vision's most pitiless disclosure: what looks from the field like strategy and valor looks from here like moths' haste. And some hang caught in the teeth — the detail no theology fully domesticates.
verses.connection: Completes 11.26: the future of the war shown as already in progress.
verses.reflection: Watch the hurry in yourself and others toward what consumes — and step out of one such rushing today.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
यथा नदीनां बहवोऽम्बुवेगाः
समुद्रमेवाभिमुखा द्रवन्ति ।
तथा तवामी नरलोकवीरा
विशन्ति वक्त्राण्यभिविज्वलन्ति॥
verses.transliteration:
yathā nadīnāṃ bahavo'mbuvegāḥ samudramevābhimukhā dravanti
tathā tavāmī naralokavīrā viśanti vaktrāṇyabhivijvalanti
verses.essence: As the many torrents of rivers race toward the ocean alone, so do these heroes of the human world enter your flaming mouths.
verses.translation: Verily, just as many torrents of rivers flow towards the ocean, even so these heroes in the world of men enter Thy flaming mouths.

anvaya.meaning: as the many water-torrents of rivers
run facing toward the ocean alone
so these heroes of the world of men
enter your mouths that blaze on every side

verses.explanation: The first of two similes, and the gentler one: rivers to the sea. It carries a strange consolation inside the horror — rivers are not destroyed by the ocean; they were always made of it, always headed there, their whole geography one long returning. Perhaps death, seen at this scale, is a homecoming wearing a catastrophe's face.
verses.connection: First reading of the rushing: inevitability as return.
verses.reflection: Try the river-reading on a loss today: not destruction, but a long-planned return to the sea.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
यथा प्रदीप्तं ज्वलनं पतङ्गा
विशन्ति नाशाय समृद्धवेगाः ।
तथैव नाशाय विशन्ति लोकास्-
तवापि वक्त्राणि समृद्धवेगाः॥
verses.transliteration:
yathā pradīptaṃ jvalanaṃ pataṅgā viśanti nāśāya samṛddhavegāḥ
tathaiva nāśāya viśanti lokāsa- tavāpi vaktrāṇi samṛddhavegāḥ
verses.essence: As moths, with quickened speed, enter a blazing fire to their destruction — so do these worlds, with quickened speed, enter your mouths to their destruction.
verses.translation: As moths hurriedly rush into a blazing fire for (their own) destruction, so also these creatures hurriedly rush into Thy mouths for (their own) destruction.

anvaya.meaning: as into a blazing fire, moths
enter, for destruction, with full speed
so too, for destruction, the worlds enter
your mouths as well, with full speed

verses.explanation: The second simile withdraws the first's comfort: not rivers now but moths — patangāḥ — whose speed toward the flame is desire itself, mistaken to the end about what the brightness is. Nāśāya, twice: *for destruction*. The same rushing, read twice — homecoming, then doom — and the vision refuses to tell Arjuna which reading is true.
verses.connection: The darker twin of 11.28: the same motion, stripped of consolation.
verses.reflection: Then try the moth-reading on a craving: speed mistaken for desire's wisdom. Hold both readings.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
लेलिह्यसे ग्रसमानः समन्ताल्-
लोकान्समग्रान्वदनैर्ज्वलद्भिः ।
तेजोभिरापूर्य जगत्समग्रं
भासस्तवोग्राः प्रतपन्ति विष्णो॥
verses.transliteration:
lelihyase grasamānaḥ samantāl- lokānsamagrānvadanairjvaladbhiḥ
tejobhirāpūrya jagatsamagraṃ bhāsastavogrāḥ pratapanti viṣṇo
verses.essence: You lick your lips, devouring all the worlds on every side with your flaming mouths. Your fierce rays fill the whole universe with radiance and scorch it, O Vishnu.
verses.translation: Thou lickest up, devouring all the worlds on every side with Thy flaming mouths. Thy fierce rays, filling the whole world with radiance, are burning, O Vishnu!

anvaya.meaning: you lick (your lips), devouring on all sides
the worlds entire, with blazing mouths
filling the whole universe with your radiance
your fierce beams scorch it, O Vishnu

verses.explanation: Lelihyase — the intensive verb: licking, relishing, again and again. The hardest word in the chapter, because it adds appetite to destruction. Arjuna addresses this as *Vishnu* — the Preserver — and the title lands like a question: the sustainer of worlds, savoring their consumption. The contradiction is now total, and unbearable.
verses.connection: The vision's nadir — beyond which only the demand of 11.31 is possible.
verses.reflection: When existence shows its devouring face, stay present anyway — the Preserver wears it too.
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
आख्याहि मे को भवानुग्ररूपो
नमोऽस्तु ते देववर प्रसीद ।
विज्ञातुमिच्छामि भवन्तमाद्यं
न हि प्रजानामि तव प्रवृत्तिम्॥
verses.transliteration:
ākhyāhi me ko bhavānugrarūpo namo'stu te devavara prasīda
vijñātumicchāmi bhavantamādyaṃ na hi prajānāmi tava pravṛttim
verses.essence: Tell me who you are, O fierce of form! Salutation to you, best of gods — be gracious. I wish to know you, the Primal One, for I do not comprehend your working.
verses.translation: Tell me, who Thou art, so fierce of form. Salutations to Thee, O God Supreme: have mercy. I desire to know Thee, the original Being. I know not indeed Thy working.

anvaya.meaning: tell me — who are you, of fierce form
salutation to you, best of gods — be gracious
I wish to know you, the Primal
for I do not understand your working

verses.explanation: The hymn ends where honest prayer often does: in a question. Kaḥ bhavān — *who are you?* — asked by the man who has spent ten chapters being told. But doctrine named the being; the vision has shown the doing, and the doing is what he cannot square: na prajānāmi tava pravṛttim, I do not understand what you are *doing*. The next verse is the answer the whole Gita has been building toward.
verses.connection: The question that summons 11.32 — identity demanded at the point where understanding breaks.
verses.reflection: Bring your hardest question to the highest address today: Who are You, and what are You doing?
verses.background: Arjuna finds words and they ascend from wonder to terror: all the gods in Your body, no beginning, middle, or end — then the blazing mouths, the worlds rushing in, the warriors of both armies streaming like rivers to the sea, like moths to flame, caught between the teeth. Trembling, he asks: who are You, of form so fierce, and what is Your purpose?

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
कालोऽस्मि लोकक्षयकृत्प्रवृद्धो
लोकान्समाहर्तुमिह प्रवृत्तः ।
ऋतेऽपि त्वां न भविष्यन्ति सर्वे
येऽवस्थिताः प्रत्यनीकेषु योधाः॥
verses.transliteration:
kālo'smi lokakṣayakṛtpravṛddho lokānsamāhartumiha pravṛttaḥ
ṛte'pi tvāṃ na bhaviṣyanti sarve ye'vasthitāḥ pratyanīkeṣu yodhāḥ
verses.essence: The Blessed Lord says: I am Time, the destroyer of worlds, grown vast, engaged here in withdrawing the worlds. Even without you, none of these warriors arrayed in the opposing ranks shall remain.
verses.translation: I am the full-grown world-destroying Time, now engaged in destroying the worlds. Even without thee, none of the warriors arrayed in the hostile armies shall live.

anvaya.meaning: I am Time, world-destroying, grown full
engaged here in gathering in the worlds

anvaya.word: ऋते अपि त्वां न भविष्यन्ति सर्वे
anvaya.meaning: even without you, they shall not be — all
the warriors stationed in the opposing armies

verses.explanation: The self-naming: kālo 'smi — I am Time. Not a god *of* time; the thing itself, the medium in which all arising is already a departing. Then the sentence that reframes Arjuna's entire crisis: ṛte 'pi tvām — *even without you* — these men are already claimed; the war's outcome is not waiting on his conscience. His choice was never whether they die. It is only what he will be while it happens. Kalo 'smi — the self-naming the world knows best, and the commentaries handle with most care. Loka-kshaya-krit pravriddhah: Time grown full, already gathering the worlds. Rite 'pi tvam — even without you: the clause that lifts authorship from Arjuna's shoulders without lifting duty. The schools agree on the sequence: first the grandiosity dies ('I would be their killer'), then the agency can act clean.
verses.connection: The answer to 11.31 and to chapter 1 at once: the deaths Arjuna dreads causing are not his to cause.
verses.schools_note: The verse Oppenheimer made famous, rendering kala as 'death.' Tradition reads it with care: Time's declaration removes Arjuna's grandiosity, not his responsibility — commentators stress it forbids both guilt ('I destroy them') and license ('nothing matters'), clearing the ground for 11.33's nimitta-matram.
verses.reflection: Let Time's self-naming resize one worry today: the outcome was never waiting on your anxiety.
verses.background: The answer that entered world history: I am Time, grown full, here to devour the worlds — even without you, these warriors will not survive. Therefore stand up, win glory, enjoy the kingdom: by Me they are already slain; be merely My instrument, O ambidextrous archer.

verses.devanagari:
तस्मात्त्वमुत्तिष्ठ यशो लभस्व
जित्वा शत्रून् भुङ्क्ष्व राज्यं समृद्धम् ।
मयैवैते निहताः पूर्वमेव
निमित्तमात्रं भव सव्यसाचिन्॥
verses.transliteration:
tasmāttvamuttiṣṭha yaśo labhasva jitvā śatrūn bhuṅkṣva rājyaṃ samṛddham
mayaivaite nihatāḥ pūrvameva nimittamātraṃ bhava savyasācin
verses.essence: Therefore arise and win glory; conquering your foes, enjoy a prospering kingdom. By me they are slain already — be merely the instrument, O ambidextrous archer.
verses.translation: Therefore, stand up and obtain fame. Coner the enemies and enjoy the unrivalled kingdom. Verily by Me have they been already slain; be thou a mere instrument, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: therefore arise, win glory
conquering foes, enjoy a thriving kingdom

anvaya.word: मया एव एते निहताः पूर्वम् एव
anvaya.meaning: by me alone are they slain, already
be merely the instrument, O Savyasachin

verses.explanation: The chapter's command, hung on its strangest tense: nihatāḥ pūrvam eva — slain *already*, in the perfect, before an arrow flies. What Time has concluded, the field will merely transcribe. Hence the famous office offered to Arjuna: nimitta-mātram, the mere occasion, the pen and not the author. The deed remains his to do; the death was never his to own. Nimitta-matram bhava savyasachin — be merely the instrument: the verse that gave Indian ethics its theory of agency under providence. Maya evaite nihatah purvam eva — slain already, by Me, beforehand: the perfect tense as theology. Commentators balance the two halves carefully: the outcome is closed, the performance is open — what remains to choose is the spirit of the hand on the bow.
verses.connection: Converts 11.32's metaphysics into 2.37's old command — now with the weight of authorship lifted.
verses.reflection: Be the instrument once today: full effort, authorship surrendered — nimitta-matram in your own field.
verses.background: The answer that entered world history: I am Time, grown full, here to devour the worlds — even without you, these warriors will not survive. Therefore stand up, win glory, enjoy the kingdom: by Me they are already slain; be merely My instrument, O ambidextrous archer.

verses.devanagari:
द्रोणं च भीष्मं च जयद्रथं च
कर्णं तथान्यानपि योधवीरान् ।
मया हतांस्त्वं जहि मा व्यथिष्ठा
युध्यस्व जेतासि रणे सपत्नान्॥
verses.transliteration:
droṇaṃ ca bhīṣmaṃ ca jayadrathaṃ ca karṇaṃ tathānyānapi yodhavīrān
mayā hatāṃstvaṃ jahi mavyathiṣṭhā yudhyasva jetāsi raṇe sapatnān
verses.essence: Drona and Bhishma and Jayadratha, Karna, and the other warrior-heroes too — already slain by me: slay them. Do not waver. Fight! You shall conquer your rivals in battle.
verses.translation: Drona, Bhishma, Jayadratha, Karna and other brave warriors these are already slain by Me: do thou kill; be not distressed with fear; fight and thou shalt coner thy enemies in battle.

anvaya.word: द्रोणं च भीष्मं च जयद्रथं च
anvaya.meaning: Drona and Bhishma and Jayadratha
Karna, and the other warrior-heroes too

anvaya.word: मया हतान् त्वं जहि मा व्यथिष्ठाः
anvaya.meaning: slain by me — you slay; do not be distressed
fight! you shall conquer your rivals in battle

verses.explanation: The names Arjuna could not face in chapter 1 are spoken back to him one by one — Drona his teacher, Bhishma his grandsire — each already filed under mayā hatān, slain by me. The paradoxical imperative follows: *you* slay the already-slain. And mā vyathiṣṭhāḥ — the same root as the worlds' trembling — do not tremble. Then a promise, flatly given: jetāsi, you will win.
verses.connection: Applies 11.33 to the exact faces of 1.26: the beloved enemies, pre-decided.
verses.reflection: Face one named fear today knowing the issue is already settled above your pay grade — and act.
verses.background: The answer that entered world history: I am Time, grown full, here to devour the worlds — even without you, these warriors will not survive. Therefore stand up, win glory, enjoy the kingdom: by Me they are already slain; be merely My instrument, O ambidextrous archer.

verses.speaker: सञ्जय उवाच
verses.devanagari:
एतच्छ्रुत्वा वचनं केशवस्य
कृताञ्जलिर्वेपमानः किरीटी ।
नमस्कृत्वा भूय एवाह कृष्णं
सगद्गदं भीतभीतः प्रणम्य॥
verses.transliteration:
etacchrutvā vacanaṃ keśavasya kṛtāñjalirvepamānaḥ kirīṭī
namaskṛtvā bhūya evāha kṛṣṇaṃ sagadgadaṃ bhītabhītaḥ praṇamya
verses.essence: Sanjaya says: hearing this word of Keshava, the crowned one, trembling and with palms joined, bowed down; terrified, prostrating, he spoke to Krishna again in a choked voice.
verses.translation: Having heard that speech of Lord Krishna, Arjuna, with joined palms, trembling, prostrating himself, again addressed Krishna, in a choked voice, bowing down, overwhelmed with fear.

anvaya.meaning: hearing this word of Keshava
with joined palms, trembling, the crowned one
bowing, again indeed spoke to Krishna
in a choked voice, terrified, prostrating

verses.explanation: Sanjaya breaks in to show us what doctrine cannot: the body of the listener. Vepamānaḥ, trembling; sa-gadgadam, the voice catching and breaking; bhīta-bhītaḥ, frightened of his own fright. The hero's epithet kirīṭī — the diademed — stands in deliberate contrast to the prostrate figure wearing it.
verses.connection: The narrative pulse-check before Arjuna's second hymn (11.36–46): awe registered in flesh and voice.
verses.reflection: Let your body pray when words fail — joined palms, bowed head, a breaking voice are all liturgy.
verses.background: Arjuna, trembling and prostrating, breaks into the Gita's great hymn: salutations in front, behind, on every side, O All. Then the most human turn — he begs pardon for every past familiarity, for 'O Krishna, O friend' spoken in play and at meals, not knowing this greatness — and pleads to see again the gentle four-armed form, for he cannot bear this one.

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
स्थाने हृषीकेश तव प्रकीर्त्या
जगत्प्रहृष्यत्यनुरज्यते च ।
रक्षांसि भीतानि दिशो द्रवन्ति
सर्वे नमस्यन्ति च सिद्धसङ्घाः॥
verses.transliteration:
sthāne hṛṣīkeśa tava prakīrtyā jagatprahṛṣyatyanurajyate ca
rakṣāṃsi bhītāni diśo dravanti sarve namasyanti ca siddhasaṅghāḥ
verses.essence: Arjuna says: rightly, Hrishikesha, does the world rejoice and delight in your praise; the rakshasas flee in terror to every quarter, and all the hosts of siddhas bow.
verses.translation: It is meet, O Krishna, that the world delights and rejoices in Thy praise; demons fly in fear to all arters and the hosts of the perfected ones bow to Thee.

anvaya.meaning: rightly, O Hrishikesha, in your glorification
the world rejoices and is enraptured
the rakshasas, terrified, flee to the directions
and all the hosts of siddhas bow

verses.explanation: Sthāne — 'rightly so', 'it is fitting': Arjuna's second hymn opens not with terror but with assent. Something has settled. The same form that routs the demonic delights the world; the response splits each being along its own grain. He is learning to praise what he could not, minutes ago, even comprehend.
verses.connection: The hymn of reconciliation begins: terror metabolizing into worship.
verses.reflection: Say 'rightly so' once today — sthane — assenting to a vastness you spent yesterday resisting.
verses.background: Arjuna, trembling and prostrating, breaks into the Gita's great hymn: salutations in front, behind, on every side, O All. Then the most human turn — he begs pardon for every past familiarity, for 'O Krishna, O friend' spoken in play and at meals, not knowing this greatness — and pleads to see again the gentle four-armed form, for he cannot bear this one.

verses.devanagari:
कस्माच्च ते न नमेरन्महात्मन्
गरीयसे ब्रह्मणोऽप्यादिकर्त्रे ।
अनन्त देवेश जगन्निवास
त्वमक्षरं सदसत्तत्परं यत्॥
verses.transliteration:
kasmācca te na nameranmahātman garīyase brahmaṇo'pyādikartre
ananta deveśa jagannivāsa tvamakṣaraṃ sadasattatparaṃ yat
verses.essence: And why should they not bow to you, O great Self — greater than Brahma, the first creator? Infinite Lord of gods, dwelling of the universe: you are the imperishable — being, non-being, and what is beyond both.
verses.translation: And why should they not, O great Soul, bow to Thee Who art greater (than all else), the primal cause even of the Creator (Brahma), O Infinite Being, O Lord of the gods, O Abode of the universe; Thou art the imperishable, the Being, the non-being and That which is the supreme (that which is beyond the Being and the non-being).

anvaya.word: कस्मात् च ते न नमेरन् महात्मन्
anvaya.meaning: and why should they not bow to you, great Self
weightier even than Brahma, the first maker
O infinite one, Lord of gods, abode of the world

anvaya.word: त्वम् अक्षरं सत् असत् तत्परं यत्
anvaya.meaning: you are the imperishable — being, non-being, and what is beyond

verses.explanation: The logic of worship made explicit: garīyase brahmaṇo 'py ādi-kartre — weightier than the world's own maker; bowing is simply accuracy. And the metaphysical reach of the line: sat, asat, tat-param yat — what is, what is not, and the third thing past both, for which philosophy keeps a respectful blank.
verses.connection: Grounds 11.36's universal homage in ontology: nothing stands outside what is being praised.
verses.reflection: Bow once today simply because bowing is accuracy before what is greater.
verses.background: Arjuna, trembling and prostrating, breaks into the Gita's great hymn: salutations in front, behind, on every side, O All. Then the most human turn — he begs pardon for every past familiarity, for 'O Krishna, O friend' spoken in play and at meals, not knowing this greatness — and pleads to see again the gentle four-armed form, for he cannot bear this one.

verses.devanagari:
त्वमादिदेवः पुरुषः पुराणस्-
त्वमस्य विश्वस्य परं निधानम् ।
वेत्तासि वेद्यं च परं च धाम
त्वया ततं विश्वमनन्तरूप॥
verses.transliteration:
tvamādidevaḥ puruṣaḥ purāṇasa- tvamasya viśvasya paraṃ nidhānam
vettāsi vedyaṃ ca paraṃ ca dhāma tvayā tataṃ viśvamanantarūpa
verses.essence: You are the first of gods, the ancient Person; you are the supreme resting-place of all this. You are the knower and the known and the highest abode. By you the universe is pervaded, O infinite form!
verses.translation: Thou art the primal God, the ancient Purusha, the supreme refuge of this universe, the knower, the knowable and the supreme Abode. By Thee is the universe pervaded, O Being of infinite forms.

anvaya.meaning: you are the first god, the ancient Person
you are the supreme repository of this universe

anvaya.word: वेत्ता असि वेद्यं च परं च धाम
anvaya.meaning: you are the knower, the to-be-known, and the supreme abode
by you the universe is pervaded, O infinite-formed

verses.explanation: The hymn gathers the Upanishadic vocabulary into one address — and hides its sharpest line in the middle: vettā asi vedyaṁ ca, you are the knower *and* the known. The seeking subject and the sought object, both him. Every search, this implies, is God looking for God — including the search trembling on this chariot.
verses.connection: Deepens 11.37: the duality of seeker and goal dissolved inside the one being addressed.
verses.reflection: Mid-search today, remember: the knower and the known are one — your seeking is being met.
verses.background: Arjuna, trembling and prostrating, breaks into the Gita's great hymn: salutations in front, behind, on every side, O All. Then the most human turn — he begs pardon for every past familiarity, for 'O Krishna, O friend' spoken in play and at meals, not knowing this greatness — and pleads to see again the gentle four-armed form, for he cannot bear this one.

verses.devanagari:
वायुर्यमोऽग्निर्वरुणः शशाङ्कः
प्रजापतिस्त्वं प्रपितामहश्च ।
नमो नमस्तेऽस्तु सहस्रकृत्वः
पुनश्च भूयोऽपि नमो नमस्ते॥
verses.transliteration:
vāyuryamo'gnirvaruṇaḥ śaśāṅkaḥ prajāpatistvaṃ prapitāmahaśca
namo namaste'stu sahasrakṛtvaḥ punaśca bhūyo'pi namo namaste
verses.essence: You are Vayu, Yama, Agni, Varuna, the moon; Prajapati are you, and the great-grandsire. Salutation, salutation to you a thousand times — and again, even more: salutation, salutation!
verses.translation: Thou art Vayu, Yama, Agni, Varuna, the moon, the Creator, and the great-grandfather. Salutations, salutations unto Thee, a thousand times, and again salutations, salutations unto Thee.

anvaya.meaning: Vayu, Yama, Agni, Varuna, the moon
you are Prajapati, and the great-grandfather
salutation, salutation to you, a thousand times

anvaya.word: पुनः च भूयः अपि नमः नमः ते
anvaya.meaning: and again, still more: salutation, salutation to you

verses.explanation: The old gods are folded in by name — wind, death, fire, waters, moon — and then language gives up its other function and becomes pure gesture: namo namas te... punaś ca bhūyo 'pi namo namas te. Four salutations in two lines, the words repeating because nothing else is left for them to do. This is what speech sounds like when it has finished meaning and started bowing.
verses.connection: The hymn's crest: vocabulary exhausted, repetition becomes the prayer.
verses.reflection: Let language become gesture once today: repeat one salutation until it stops meaning and starts bowing.
verses.background: Arjuna, trembling and prostrating, breaks into the Gita's great hymn: salutations in front, behind, on every side, O All. Then the most human turn — he begs pardon for every past familiarity, for 'O Krishna, O friend' spoken in play and at meals, not knowing this greatness — and pleads to see again the gentle four-armed form, for he cannot bear this one.

verses.devanagari:
नमः पुरस्तादथ पृष्ठतस्ते
नमोऽस्तु ते सर्वत एव सर्व ।
अनन्तवीर्यामितविक्रमस्त्वं
सर्वं समाप्नोषि ततोऽसि सर्वः॥
verses.transliteration:
namaḥ purastādatha pṛṣṭhataste namo'stu te sarvata eva sarva
anantavīryāmitavikramastvaṃ sarvaṃ samāpnoṣi tato'si sarvaḥ
verses.essence: Salutation to you from the front and from behind; salutation to you on every side, O All! Of infinite might and immeasurable strength, you pervade everything — and therefore you are everything.
verses.translation: Salutations to Thee, in front and behind! Salutations to Thee on every side! O All!! Thou infinite in power and prowess, pervadest all; wherefore Thou art all.

anvaya.meaning: salutation from the front, and from behind, to you

anvaya.word: नमः अस्तु ते सर्वतः एव सर्व
anvaya.meaning: salutation to you from every side, O All
of infinite power and measureless valor, you
you pervade all — therefore you are all

verses.explanation: Geometry returns one last time, reconciled: bowing front, back, every side — the directions Arjuna lost in 11.25 are restored as so many angles of homage. And the hymn closes its argument with a syllogism of two words: samāpnoṣi, you fill everything; tato 'si sarvaḥ — *therefore you are everything*. Pervasion, thought through, is identity.
verses.connection: Completes the praise-hymn — and pivots to what total presence implies about an old friendship.
verses.reflection: Bow in all directions inwardly once — front, behind, every side — there is no away from this.
verses.background: Arjuna, trembling and prostrating, breaks into the Gita's great hymn: salutations in front, behind, on every side, O All. Then the most human turn — he begs pardon for every past familiarity, for 'O Krishna, O friend' spoken in play and at meals, not knowing this greatness — and pleads to see again the gentle four-armed form, for he cannot bear this one.

verses.devanagari:
सखेति मत्वा प्रसभं यदुक्तं
हे कृष्ण हे यादव हे सखेति ।
अजानता महिमानं तवेदं
मया प्रमादात्प्रणयेन वापि॥
verses.transliteration:
sakheti matvā prasabhaṃ yaduktaṃ he kṛṣṇa he yādava he sakheti
ajānatā mahimānaṃ tavedaṃ mayā pramādātpraṇayena vāpi
verses.essence: Whatever I said rashly, thinking of you as friend — 'O Krishna! O Yadava! O comrade!' — unknowing of this your greatness, out of carelessness or even out of love —
verses.translation: Whatever I have presumptuously said from carelessness or love, addressing Thee as O Krishna! O Yadava! O Friend! regarding Thee merely as a friend, unknowing of this, Thy greatness.

anvaya.word: सखा इति मत्वा प्रसभं यत् उक्तं
anvaya.meaning: thinking 'a friend', whatever was rashly said

anvaya.word: हे कृष्ण हे यादव हे सखे इति
anvaya.meaning: 'O Krishna! O Yadava! O friend!' — thus
not knowing this greatness of yours
by me — from carelessness, or even from affection

verses.explanation: The most human turn in the chapter: Arjuna replaying decades of casual address — hey Krishna, hey Yadava — every easy word now enormous in retrospect. His honesty about the cause is exact: pramādāt praṇayena vāpi, from heedlessness *or from love*. He cannot tell which, because in friendship they are the same thing.
verses.connection: Begins the apology (11.41–44): intimacy auditing itself before the revealed infinite.
verses.reflection: Review your casual words to those you love — and let retrospect deepen, not poison, the affection.
verses.background: Arjuna, trembling and prostrating, breaks into the Gita's great hymn: salutations in front, behind, on every side, O All. Then the most human turn — he begs pardon for every past familiarity, for 'O Krishna, O friend' spoken in play and at meals, not knowing this greatness — and pleads to see again the gentle four-armed form, for he cannot bear this one.

verses.devanagari:
यच्चावहासार्थमसत्कृतोऽसि
विहारशय्यासनभोजनेषु ।
एकोऽथवाप्यच्युत तत्समक्षं
तत्क्षामये त्वामहमप्रमेयम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yaccāvahāsārthamasatkṛto'si vihāraśayyāsanabhojaneṣu
eko'thavāpyacyuta tatsamakṣaṃ tatkṣāmaye tvāmahamaprameyam
verses.essence: — and however I slighted you in jest: at play, in bed, seated or at meals, alone or before others, O unshakable one — for that I beg forgiveness of you, the immeasurable.
verses.translation: In whatever way I may have insulted Thee for the sake of fun, while at play, reposing, sitting or at meals, when alone (with Thee), O Krishna, or in company that I implore Thee, immeasurable one, to forgive.

anvaya.meaning: and however, for the sake of jest, you were slighted
in play, on the bed, while seated, at meals
alone, or even, O Achyuta, before others
for that I ask forgiveness of you, the immeasurable

verses.explanation: The inventory of intimacy: play, bed, seat, table — the four geographies of friendship, every one now reviewed under infinite light. The verse holds the Gita's tenderest paradox: only a God who had truly been a *friend* — teased, jostled, kept waiting at dinner — could need this apology. The incarnation was so complete it accumulated inside jokes.
verses.connection: Completes 11.41's confession with its domestic particulars — the divinity of the shared meal.
verses.reflection: Apologize today for one old carelessness that only love would even remember.
verses.background: Arjuna, trembling and prostrating, breaks into the Gita's great hymn: salutations in front, behind, on every side, O All. Then the most human turn — he begs pardon for every past familiarity, for 'O Krishna, O friend' spoken in play and at meals, not knowing this greatness — and pleads to see again the gentle four-armed form, for he cannot bear this one.

verses.devanagari:
पितासि लोकस्य चराचरस्य
त्वमस्य पूज्यश्च गुरुर्गरीयान् ।
न त्वत्समोऽस्त्यभ्यधिकः कुतोऽन्यो
लोकत्रयेऽप्यप्रतिमप्रभाव॥
verses.transliteration:
pitāsi lokasya carācarasya tvamasya pūjyaśca gururgarīyān
na tvatsamo'styabhyadhikaḥ kuto'nyo lokatraye'pyapratimaprabhāva
verses.essence: You are the father of this world, of the moving and unmoving; you are its object of worship, its weightiest guru. None equals you — how could any be greater, O power without likeness in all three worlds?
verses.translation: Thou art the Father of this world, moving and unmoving. Thou art to be adored by this world, Thou, the greatest Guru; (for) none there exists who is eal to Thee; how then could there be another superior to Thee in the three worlds, O Being of unealled power?

anvaya.meaning: you are the father of the world, moving and unmoving

anvaya.word: त्वम् अस्य पूज्यः च गुरुः गरीयान्
anvaya.meaning: you are its worshipful one, and its weightiest guru

anvaya.word: न त्वत्समः अस्ति अभ्यधिकः कुतः अन्यः
anvaya.meaning: none is your equal — how then another greater
even in the three worlds, O matchless might

verses.explanation: The relationship is formally re-founded: pitā, pūjyaḥ, gurur garīyān — father, worshipful, weightiest teacher. The friend has not been lost but re-housed inside something vaster. And the logic of incomparability: where nothing is even equal, 'greater' is not a possibility but a grammatical error.
verses.connection: The apology resolves into right relation: friendship preserved, but re-ordered under reverence.
verses.schools_note: 'Na tvat-samo 'sty abhyadhikah kuto 'nyah' — none equals You; how could any exceed? The Vaishnava schools cite this as the Gita's formal statement of God's unsurpassability, foundational for Ramanuja's definition of the Lord as that than which nothing greater exists. Advaita reads the incomparability as pointing past all relation. Both note who says it: the friend, mid-apology — supremacy confessed by intimacy.
verses.reflection: Re-house a beloved relationship inside reverence today — friend, and father, and teacher at once.
verses.background: Arjuna, trembling and prostrating, breaks into the Gita's great hymn: salutations in front, behind, on every side, O All. Then the most human turn — he begs pardon for every past familiarity, for 'O Krishna, O friend' spoken in play and at meals, not knowing this greatness — and pleads to see again the gentle four-armed form, for he cannot bear this one.

verses.devanagari:
तस्मात्प्रणम्य प्रणिधाय कायं
प्रसादये त्वामहमीशमीड्यम् ।
पितेव पुत्रस्य सखेव सख्युः
प्रियः प्रियायार्हसि देव सोढुम्॥
verses.transliteration:
tasmātpraṇamya praṇidhāya kāyaṃ prasādaye tvāmahamīśamīḍyam
piteva putrasya sakheva sakhyuḥ priyaḥ priyāyārhasi deva soḍhum
verses.essence: Therefore, bowing and prostrating my body, I ask grace of you, the Lord, the praiseworthy. As a father with a son, a friend with a friend, a lover with the beloved — so should you bear with me, O God.
verses.translation: Therefore, bowing down, prostrating my body, I crave Thy forgiveness, O adorable Lord. As a father forgives his son, a friend his (dear) friend, a lover his beloved, even so shouldst Thou forgive me, O God.

anvaya.meaning: therefore, bowing, laying down my body
I seek the grace of you, the Lord, the adorable

anvaya.word: पिता इव पुत्रस्य सखा इव सख्युः
anvaya.meaning: as a father a son's (fault), as a friend a friend's
as a lover the beloved's — so should you bear, O God

verses.explanation: Having confessed the presumption of intimacy, Arjuna does something braver: he asks for it back. Three analogies, ascending in tenderness — father-son, friend-friend, lover-beloved — each a relation in which faults are not merely excused but *absorbed*. He is asking the Infinite to keep loving him the small way, the personal way. It is the boldest request in the chapter, bolder than asking to see the form.
verses.connection: The apology's astonishing conclusion: forgiveness sought in the very currency of the offense — closeness.
verses.reflection: Ask to be borne with as lover bears beloved — and bear with someone else the same way today.
verses.background: Arjuna, trembling and prostrating, breaks into the Gita's great hymn: salutations in front, behind, on every side, O All. Then the most human turn — he begs pardon for every past familiarity, for 'O Krishna, O friend' spoken in play and at meals, not knowing this greatness — and pleads to see again the gentle four-armed form, for he cannot bear this one.

verses.devanagari:
अदृष्टपूर्वं हृषितोऽस्मि दृष्ट्वा
भयेन च प्रव्यथितं मनो मे ।
तदेव मे दर्शय देव रूपं
प्रसीद देवेश जगन्निवास॥
verses.transliteration:
adṛṣṭapūrvaṃ hṛṣito'smi dṛṣṭvā bhayena ca pravyathitaṃ mano me
tadeva me darśaya deva rūpaṃ prasīda deveśa jagannivāsa
verses.essence: Having seen what was never seen before, I am thrilled — and my mind is shaken with fear. Show me that other form, O God; be gracious, Lord of gods, dwelling of the universe.
verses.translation: I am delighted, having seen what has never been seen before; and yet my mind is distressed with fear. Show me that (previous) form only, O God; have mercy, O God of gods, O Abode of the universe.

anvaya.meaning: having seen the never-before-seen, I am thrilled
and with fear my mind is shaken

anvaya.word: तत् एव मे दर्शय देव रूपं
anvaya.meaning: that very form show me, O God
be gracious, Lord of gods, abode of the world

verses.explanation: Both truths in one breath: hṛṣito 'smi — I am exhilarated — *and* my mind shakes with fear; the vision was gift and wound at once. Then the request that reverses the chapter: tad eva me darśaya rūpam — show me *that* form, the old one, the one with two arms and a familiar smile. Having seen everything, Arjuna asks for the face he loves.
verses.connection: The counter-request to 11.3: the petition to close what he begged to open.
verses.reflection: Permit both at once today: thrilled and afraid. Then ask, without shame, for the gentler face.
verses.background: Arjuna, trembling and prostrating, breaks into the Gita's great hymn: salutations in front, behind, on every side, O All.
